title: Interferometric method and apparatus for non-invasive assessment of oocyte maturity and competency
abstract: An interferometric method and apparatus for the non-invasive assessment of oocyte maturity and competency. The method includes placing an oocyte (82) in a sample holder (64) to provide a biological target (34); generating a near infrared light (38); using a beam splitter (40) to split the near infrared light into a signal light portion (60) and a reference light portion (62); projecting the signal light portion onto the biological target; collecting reflected and back scattered light from the signal light portion projected onto the biological target with a detector (46); collecting at least a portion of the reference light portion with the detector; generating interferometric image data based upon the collected signal and reference light; and assessing the maturity of the oocyte based upon the interferometric data while maintaining the viability of the oocyte. When the oocyte is part of a cumulus-oocyte complex, the assessment is accomplished without removing cumulus cells from the cumulus-oocyte complex while maintaining viability.
claims:
Claims WHAT IS CLAIMED IS: 1. A method for non-invasively assessing the maturity of an oocyte (82), the method comprising: placing the oocyte (82) in a sample holder (64) to provide a biological target (34); generating a near infrared light (38) with a light source (30); using a beam splitter (40) to split the near infrared light (38) into a signal light portion (60) and a reference light portion (62); projecting the signal light portion of the near infrared light onto the biological target; collecting reflected and back scattered light from the signal light portion projected onto the biological target with a detector (46); collecting at least a portion of the reference light portion with the detector wherein the collected reference light has not interacted with the biological target; generating interferometric image data based upon the collected signal and reference light; and assessing the maturity of the oocyte based upon the interferometric data while maintaining the viability of the oocyte. 2. The method of claim 1 wherein the oocyte forming the biological target is part of a cumulus- oocyte complex and the step of assessing of the maturity of the oocyte is accomplished without denuding cumulus cells (84) from the cumulus-oocyte complex while maintaining the viability of the cumulus-oocyte complex. 3. The method of any one of claims 1 or 2 wherein the near infrared light (38) generated by the light source (30) has a wavelength within the range of 800 micrometers to 1000 micrometers. 4. The method of claim 3 wherein the near infrared light (38) generated by the light source (30) has a wavelength of 850 micrometers. 5. The method of claim 3 wherein the near infrared light (38) generated by the light source (30) has a power of no more than 5 milliwatts and the step of projecting the signal light portion of the near infrared light onto the biological target (34) exposes the biological target to no more than 

100 millijoules of energy from the near infrared light. 6. The method of claim 5 wherein the light source (30) used to generate the near infrared light is a superluminescent diode. 7. The method of claim 6 wherein the superluminescent diode (30) is coupled with a fiber optic cable (32) and the method further comprises using the fiber optic cable to convey the near infrared light from the superluminescent diode to a location (36) where the light is projected at the beam splitter. 8. The method of claim 7 wherein the superluminescent diode and driving circuitry (28) providing the superluminescent diode (30) with an electrical current to generate the near infrared light are positioned in a first housing (31) that is spaced from and separated from a probe housing (21), the beam splitter (40) and detector (46) being disposed within the probe housing (21) and wherein the fiber optic cable (32) extends from the first housing (31) to the probe housing (21) and wherein the first housing includes a cooling fan (54) and the probe housing does not include a cooling fan. 9. The method of claim 6 further comprising the step of providing an objective lens (44) in the path of the signal light (60) between the beam splitter (40) and the biological target (34). 10. The method of claim 9 further comprising the step of providing a pair of galvanometric mirrors (42) which are controllably rotated about a pair of mutually perpendicular axes and positioning the galvanometric mirrors in the path of the signal light (60) between the beam splitter (40) and the objective lens (44) whereby controlled rotation of the galvanometric mirrors can be used to scan the biological target (34) with the signal light. 11. The method of claim 6 further comprising the step of providing a second image sensor (50) for recording two-dimensional image data. 12. The method of claim 11 wherein a plurality of biological targets (34) are assessed, the 

 plurality of biological targets being disposed in a plurality of sample holders (64) wherein each individual sample holder has an identifying label (66) affixed thereto, and wherein the method further comprises acquiring an image of the identifying label (66) with the second image sensor (50) when assessing a biological target from the sample holder. 13. The method of claim 11 further comprising the step of providing an objective lens (44) in the path of the signal light (60) between the beam splitter (40) and the biological target (34) and positioning the second image sensor (50) to acquire two-dimensional image data of the biological target through the objective lens (44). 14. The method of claim 3 further comprising: providing an objective lens (44) in the path of the signal light (60) between the beam splitter (40) and the biological target (34); mounting the objective lens (44), the beam splitter (40) and the detector (46) in a probe housing (21); positioning the sample holder (64) on a target support (76); and providing a support structure (68) wherein the probe housing (21) is securable to the support structure (68) at a plurality of different locations whereby the signal light can be directed at the biological target from a plurality of different positions. 15. A system (20) for non-invasively assessing the maturity of an oocyte (82), the system comprising: a superluminescent diode (30) which generates a near infrared light (38) having a wavelength within the range of 800 micrometers to 1000 micrometers; a beam splitter (40) positioned to divide the near infrared light (38) and thereby generate a signal light portion (60) and a reference light portion (62), the signal light portion being directed at a biological target (34) to generate reflected and back scattered signal light; a reference beam reflector (48) positioned to reflect the reference light portion; a detector (46) positioned to receive the reference light portion reflected by the reference beam reflector and the reflected and back scattered signal light to thereby generate interferometric image data of the biological target. 16. The system of claim 15 wherein the near infrared light (38) generated by the superluminescent diode (30) has a wavelength of 850 micrometers. 17. The system of claim 15 wherein the superluminescent diode (30) is coupled with a fiber optic cable (32), the fiber optic cable conveying the near infrared light generated by the superluminescent diode to a location (36) where the light is projected at the beam splitter (40). 18. The system of claim 17 further comprising driving circuitry (28) which supplies electrical current to drive the operation of the superluminescent diode (30), the driving circuitry and the superluminescent diode being disposed in a first housing (31), the beam splitter (40), the reference beam reflector (48) and the detector (46) being disposed in a probe housing (21), the probe housing (21) being separate and spaced apart from the first housing (31), the fiber optic cable (32) conveying the near infrared light extending between the first housing and the probe housing and emitting the near infrared light within the probe housing. 19. The system of claim 15 wherein the system further comprises: a pair of galvanometric mirrors (42) controllably rotatable about mutually perpendicular axes wherein the galvanometric mirrors are positioned in the path of the signal light portion (60) between the beam splitter (40) and the biological target (34); and an objective lens (44) positioned in the path of the signal light portion between the pair of galvanometric mirrors (42) and the biological target (34). 20. The system of claim 15 further comprising a second image sensor (50) adapted to record two-dimensional image data. 21. The system of claim 20 further comprising a plurality of sample holders (64), each sample holder being adapted to hold a separate biological target (34) and wherein each sample holder has an identifying label (66) affixed thereto, and wherein the second image sensor (50) is adapted to acquire an image of the identifying label (66) on the sample holder. 22. The system of claim 20 further comprising an objective lens (44) positioned between the beam splitter (40) and the biological target (34) and a second light source (52) positioned to illuminate the biological target and wherein the second image sensor (50) is positioned to collect light (53) generated by the second light source (52) after the light generated by the second light source has interacted with the biological target and passed through the objective lens (44) to thereby acquire image data of the biological target. 23. The system of claim 15 further comprising a probe housing (21) and an objective lens (44) wherein the beam splitter (40), reference beam reflector (48), objective lens (44) and detector (46) are disposed on the probe housing (21) and wherein the objective lens (44) is positioned in the path of the signal light portion (60) between the beam splitter (40) and the biological target (34); and a support structure (68), the support structure comprising a support ring (74) and a target support (76), the biological target (34) being supportable on the target support (76), and wherein the probe housing is securable to the support ring (74) at a plurality of different location encircling the target support whereby the signal light can be directed at the biological target from a plurality of different positions. 24. The system of any one of claims 17-23 wherein the near infrared light (38) generated by the superluminescent diode (30) has a power of no more than 5 milliwatts as conveyed through the fiber optic cable (32).
